natural_occurrence.description: A colorless, sweet-smelling gas with mild anesthetic properties. Best known as 'laughing gas' in dentistry, but widely used in food industry as a propellant for whipped cream and aerosol products.

manufacturing.description: Produced industrially by carefully controlled thermal decomposition of ammonium nitrate at 240-270°C. The gas is purified, compressed, and stored in steel cylinders for food and medical use.

other_uses.cosmetics: Aerosol propellant in hair spray and other sprays
other_uses.industrial: Rocket propellant, race car engine booster, semiconductor manufacturing
other_uses.household: Whipped cream chargers (N2O cartridges), automotive performance

eu.conditions: Authorized as propellant and packaging gas

usa.notes: Approved for direct addition to food as propellant and aerating agent

canada.reference: Lists of Permitted Carrier or Extraction Solvents

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: Not specified (no safety concern for food use)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: Not specified (no toxicological concern at food use levels)

regulatory_history.event: Horace Wells uses nitrous oxide as dental anesthetic
Nitrous oxide approved for use in whipped cream dispensers
UK and other countries implement age restrictions on N2O sales due to recreational abuse

adi_context.source_note: No ADI needed for food use - nitrous oxide disperses immediately from food products and is not consumed in significant amounts.

fun_facts: Also known as 'laughing gas' - used in dentistry since 1844
The propellant in your whipped cream can
Causes euphoria when inhaled (recreational abuse is illegal)
Also used as a performance booster in race cars ('NOS')
Makes whipped cream fluffy by dissolving in fat and expanding
Potent greenhouse gas - 300x more powerful than CO2
